peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low openness and high authoritarianism drive skepticism of mandated transparency as intrusive regulation, while high conscientiousness and system justification temper the opposition to a moderate level.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation combined with very high agreeableness and trust in science produce moderate support, but very low openness tempers enthusiasm for complex algorithmic regulation.
High openness and internal political efficacy drive support for transparency, but low trust in institutions and moderate libertarian anti-censorship views temper enthusiasm to a mild level.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High agreeableness and strong care-harm moral foundation drive support for transparency to protect vulnerable users, but very low openness and low libertarianism temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness and agreeableness, combined with strong fairness/cheating concerns (low) and high care/harm, lead to moderate support for transparency as a protective measure, but libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) tempers it.

Despite very low trust in institutions and a libertarian skepticism of state control, the person's high moral concern for harm avoidance and strong sense of accountability (justice value) drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency as a protective measure against manipulation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High extraversion and civic engagement support disclosure to protect social trust, but low trust in institutions and high collective narcissism limit enthusiasm for government-led enforcement.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.
Strong moral concern for fairness and high conscientiousness drive support for disclosure, though moderate populist attitudes and low openness reduce the intensity.

Strong loyalty/ingroup morality and a dangerous worldview make this person want to expose inauthentic actors, yet low digital literacy and intermittent access reduce personal urgency to a clear but not strong support.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.
Strong anti-elitism and populist attitudes align with exposing deceptive actors, while high conscientiousness and moderate authoritarian submission support clear labeling rules.

High care/harm (87.29) and moderate social dominance orientation (73.96) create a cautious endorsement of disclosure to protect vulnerable users, though low libertarian privacy (20.54) reduces full enthusiasm.

The combination of a dangerous worldview, high threat sensitivity, and a strong care/harm foundation leads to clear support for disclosing inauthentic behavior to protect the public from coordinated deception.

High agreeableness and trust in government favor regulation, but extremely high anti-censorship orientation and libertarianism create countervailing skepticism, yielding mild support.

Moderate authoritarian submission and a dangerous worldview lean toward regulation, but low trust in institutions, moderate libertarian skepticism of state control, and a dismissive-avoidant style produce near-neutral mild opposition.

Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.
High agreeableness and care-harm values drive support for accountability, while low trust in institutions and moderate authoritarian submission create a tempered but clear stance.

Low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high system justification (85.5) create mild opposition to heavy regulation, preferring market-based solutions, though high care/harm (87.29) provides some counterweight.
Despite a dangerous worldview, very low authoritarianism and strong anti-domination liberty foundation create mild opposition to formal regulation, preferring market or community solutions.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extreme distrust in institutions and very low trust in media, combined with high economic conservatism, drive strong opposition to funding public broadcasting.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) oppose state-funded media, reinforced by very low agreeableness (0.61) and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) that may view public broadcasting as elite-driven.
Very high economic left-wing orientation (98.49), support for public sector role, and high trust in government and experts strongly favor public service broadcasting.
Moderate economic conservatism on public sector role and low trust in government lead to mild opposition, despite high openness to diverse content and a philanthropic orientation.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
Low trust in mainstream journalism and high anti-elitism produce mild opposition, partially offset by high agreeableness and care for community information needs.

Moderate trust in institutions (56.85) and high altruism (92.88) yield mild support for public broadcasting as a common good, but low trust in government (42.45) and high libertarian skepticism (10.55) limit enthusiasm.

Very low trust in mainstream institutions and a libertarian skepticism of state-controlled media produce slight opposition, but the philanthropist drive for prosocial outcomes prevents strong rejection.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness and high authoritarianism oppose mandated user choice as unnecessary regulation, though high conscientiousness slightly moderates the opposition.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.
High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation oppose mandates, but concern for platform accountability and high agreeableness lead to only mild opposition.
High openness and a free-spirit HEXAD profile support user autonomy in algorithmic choice, while moderate libertarian anti-censorship aligns with this, yielding clear support.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
Very low openness and low digital literacy lead to near-neutral ambivalence, as the concept is unfamiliar and the person lacks strong opinion on algorithmic choice.

High openness (69.03) and moderate free-spirit autonomy (46.95) support user choice in algorithms, but low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high agreeableness (77.08) create a mild, cautious endorsement.
High free-spirit user type and a value for autonomy, combined with a dangerous worldview that fears algorithmic manipulation, produce clear support for giving users control over their feeds.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and order preference support labeling for clarity, but low trust in enforcers and high system justification keep support mild.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).
High trust in science and concern for information integrity support labeling, but libertarian skepticism of mandates and very low openness temper support to mild.
A dangerous worldview and high conscientiousness (order) strongly favor labeling to prevent deception, while high openness to new tech makes the mandate seem practical, resulting in clear support.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.
Strong care-harm and sanctity-purity foundations, combined with high conscientiousness, produce clear support for labeling to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
High care/harm (87.29) and strong fairness/cheating (13.89) drive clear support for labelling to prevent deception, while low authoritarian submission (19.56) ensures it is not seen as overreach.
Strong care/harm foundation and a dangerous worldview that sees deepfakes as a threat, combined with a value for honesty, drive clear support for mandatory labeling of synthetic media.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely low trust in institutions, high collective narcissism, and populist anti-elitism produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite institution.

Very low agreeableness (0.61), low trust in institutions (42.66), and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) combine with low openness to experience (22.69) to produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.

High trust in government and institutions (74.62) and low populist attitudes and low competitive jungle worldview produce moderate trust, but very low openness and moderate collective narcissism prevent strong trust.
Low trust in institutions, moderate anti-elitism, and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism, despite moderate trust in science and experts.

Extremely low trust in science (2.57) and government (7.77) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-of-touch institution.
Low trust in science and experts, high anti-elitism, and personal experience of discrimination produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.

Low trust in science/expert (47.1) and moderate populist anti-elitism (15.09) create mild distrust of mainstream journalism, though high openness (69.03) and moderate political trust (56.85) prevent full rejection.
Extremely low trust in all institutions (government, judiciary, police, science) and high anti-elitism produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism, consistent with a competitive worldview.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High extraversion and civic engagement support protections for exposing wrongdoing, though high authoritarianism and system justification limit this to moderate support.

High libertarian anti-domination (90.15) and resistance to bullying/tyranny (95.39) strongly support whistleblower protections, while high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) creates some hesitation about destabilizing platforms.
Strong moral foundations on fairness and very high agreeableness combined with high trust in science produce clear support for protecting researchers.

High internal political efficacy and a philanthropic drive for knowledge sharing strongly support protections, while moderate libertarian anti-surveillance and low trust in institutions add caution, yielding clear support.

High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.
High agreeableness and fairness values support protecting truth-tellers, while low openness and moderate authoritarian submission limit full endorsement.

High altruism (92.88) and strong fairness/cheating (13.89) drive strong support for protections to expose wrongdoing, while low authoritarian submission (19.56) and high openness (69.03) align with this.
High honesty-humility and a strong value for accountability, combined with a dangerous worldview that sees hidden harms, produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on power.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely low trust in government and institutions, combined with high populist attitudes and collective narcissism, produce very strong distrust of election integrity.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.

Very high system justification (88.19), trust in government (88.17), and very low populist attitudes (30.65) combined with low competitive jungle worldview produce strong trust in elections, i.e., low distrust.
Moderate trust in government and low populist attitudes reduce distrust, but a dismissive-avoidant style and past justice system contact create mild skepticism, resulting in near-neutral mild trust.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Low system justification and moderate external political efficacy produce mild distrust, but high conventionalism and authoritarian submission prevent strong endorsement of election distrust.

High system justification (85.5) and moderate trust in government (42.45) produce clear opposition to distrusting elections, though low political trust (42.45) and moderate populist attitudes (31.61) create some ambivalence.
Extremely low trust in government and judiciary, high anti-elitism, a dangerous worldview, and a history of discrimination exposure combine to produce strong distrust of electoral integrity.
